coredata.dc:title: Indentation of small format Lithium-ion pouch cells: Experiments and modeling
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Solids and Structures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: Special Issue in the honour Dr Stelios Kyriakides
coredata.dc:creator: Tancogne-Dejean, Thomas
Mohr, Dirk
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion pouch cells are widely used in applications such as cell phones and electric vehicle battery packs. Here, a series of indentation experiments is performed on 4Ah pouch cells. The experimental program includes hemispherical indentation with six different indenter tip radii as well as indentation with cylindrical indenters with three rod diameters. Aside from varying the shape and size of the indenters, the location and direction of loading is varied including out-of-plane and in-plane loading of the rectangular-prismatic pouch cells. It is found that the force–displacement curves obtained for hemispherical indenters scale linearly with the indenter tip radius, while a scaling with the square root of the radius is observed for the experiments with cylindrical indenters. Finite element simulations are performed of all experiments using an enhanced Deshpande-Fleck plasticity model with two stage hardening. Despite the orthotropic nature of the jelly-stack, it is found that the simulations with an isotropic plasticity model provide reasonable predictions of the force–displacement curves for all experimental configurations. This surprising result is explained through the exceptionally high slenderness of the metallic current collector foils comprised in a pouch cell that respond through early buckling failure instead of contributing to the in-plane compressive load carrying capacity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion battery
Porous plasticity
Indentation
Finite element analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Paul Meyer (ETH) for his help in performing the experiments. Dr. Christian Roth (ETH) and Prof. Vincent Grolleau (ETH) are thanked for valuable discussions. The support of MIT's Industrial Battery Consortium is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sketch of the pouch cell and loading cases: (a) top view and (b) dimetric view, where the black dots indicate the indentation positions. (c) out-of-plane hemispherical (d) cylindrical indentations and (e) in-plane hemispherical indentations.

Lithium-ion pouch cell materials. (a) Cross-sectional view of the jelly stack with ① the pouch cell, ② the anode with the copper current collector and ③ the anode current collector. Scanning Electron Micrographs of jelly stack materials. (b) separator with submicron-sized porosities. (c) active material and (d) graphite.

Punches used for the out-of-plane indentation. From left to right
R =
2.5 , 5 , 10 , 15 , 25 , 50
m m
. The smallest punch is also used for in-plane indentation.
Calibrated two-stage hardening law.
Undeformed meshes for (a) out-of-plane and (b) in-plane indentation.

Experimental results for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with increasing radii from (a)
R = 2.5 m m
to (f)
R = 50 m m
.

Position dependency of the force–displacement response for the out-of-plane indentation with radius (a)
R = 2.5 m m
and (b)
R = 5 m m
.
(a) Normalized force–displacement response under out-of-plane hemispherical indentation. Evolution of (b) the maximum force and (c) the critical displacement as a function of the indenter radius.

Deformed views under out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with a radius
R = 10 m m
. (a) to (d) vertical displacement of the cell obtained from 3D-DIC and (e) deformed profile of the bottom side of the cell.
Evolution of the top surface of the cell: (a) vertical displacement, (b) flatness and (c) angle to indenter axis.
Out-of-plane cylindrical indentation with an indenter radius of (a) 5 mm, (b) 10 mm and (c) 15 mm. (d) Normalized force–displacement curves for all three radii.

Deformation of the cell under cylindrical out-of-plane indentation. (a)-(d) Deformed cells at increased displacement with superimposed vertical displacement profile. (e) Corresponding mid-section vertical displacement. The cyan cross delimits the region used for linear fitting (represented as cyan dashed lines). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
In-plane hemispherical indentation. The out-of-plane indentation (black line) is shown for comparison purposes.

Selected micrographs of the out-of-plane and in-plane hemispherical indentations. (a) to (f) out-of-plane indentations with radii
R =
2.5 , 5 , 10 , 15 , 25 , 50
m m
and in-plane indentation along the (g) long and (h) short cell side.

Numerical results for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with increasing radii from (a)
R = 2.5 m m
to (f)
R = 50 m m
.

Numerical results for the out-of-plane cylindrical indentation with increasing radii from (a)
R = 5 m m
to (f)
R = 15 m m
.
Numerical results for both in-plane hemispherical indentations.

Deformed meshes for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with increasing radii from (a)
R = 2.5 m m
to (f)
R = 50 m m
. The colormap represents the equivalent stress.

Deformed meshes for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with radius
R = 10 m m
at various displacement level (a,c,e) and for out-of-plane cylindrical indentation with radius
R = 10 m m
. The displacement level corresponds to Fig. 6c and Fig. 11b respectively. The colormap represents the equivalent stress.

Deformed meshes for the out-of-plane cylindrical indentation with increasing radii from (a)
R = 5 m m
to (f)
R = 15 m m
. The colormap represents the equivalent stress.
Deformed meshes for the in-plane hemispherical indentations along both sides of the cells at a displacement of 4.2 mm. The colormap represents the equivalent stress.

Evolution of the strain state at the top of the cell right under the punch and at the half thickness. (a)-(b) Triaxiality for the hemispherical and cylindrical out-of-plane indentation and (c)-(d) Lode angle parameter.
Calibrated material model parameters.

head.ce:title: Indentation of small format Lithium-ion pouch cells: Experiments and modeling

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology, ETH, 8005 Zürich, Switzerland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering, Swiss Federal Institute of Technology, ETH, 8005 Zürich, Switzerland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion pouch cells are widely used in applications such as cell phones and electric vehicle battery packs. Here, a series of indentation experiments is performed on 4Ah pouch cells. The experimental program includes hemispherical indentation with six different indenter tip radii as well as indentation with cylindrical indenters with three rod diameters. Aside from varying the shape and size of the indenters, the location and direction of loading is varied including out-of-plane and in-plane loading of the rectangular-prismatic pouch cells. It is found that the force–displacement curves obtained for hemispherical indenters scale linearly with the indenter tip radius, while a scaling with the square root of the radius is observed for the experiments with cylindrical indenters. Finite element simulations are performed of all experiments using an enhanced Deshpande-Fleck plasticity model with two stage hardening. Despite the orthotropic nature of the jelly-stack, it is found that the simulations with an isotropic plasticity model provide reasonable predictions of the force–displacement curves for all experimental configurations. This surprising result is explained through the exceptionally high slenderness of the metallic current collector foils comprised in a pouch cell that respond through early buckling failure instead of contributing to the in-plane compressive load carrying capacity.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries account for a significant weight and volume fraction of engineered products such as cell phones and electric vehicles. The computer-aided engineering of these products requires reliable computational models that are able to predict the mechanical load carrying capacity of batteries, thereby enabling engineers to optimize the overall structural response in case of accidental impact or crash loading. Furthermore, models are needed that predict the possible short-circuit associated with the mechanical abuse of batteries. Batteries typically come in cylindrical or prismatic form. While cylindrical cells (e.g. jelly-roll with steel casing) are well standardized and being mass-produced for decades, prismatic cells are favored in volume-sensitive applications due to their higher packaging density. In particular, pouch cells are popular as they come with a lightweight, soft shell casing. They are often produced in the form of a rectangular prism, where the thickness direction of the battery lay-up corresponds to the height direction of the prism. Rectangular pouch cells are widely used in cell phones, laptop computers and electric vehicles.

Zhu et al. (2018a) provide a comprehensive review on the mechanical testing of batteries. Out-of-plane indentation with hemispherical, cylindrical, flat, or wedge indenters appears to be the most relevant mechanical loading scenario for pouch cells. Further lab-type of loading scenarios include pinching (i.e. the compression between two spheres) and three-point bending. Wierzbicki and co-workers (Chung et al., 2018; Sahraei et al., 2014) investigated the hemispherical indentations response of pouch cells and reported an increase in force with increasing punch radii as well as an increasing displacement at failure. Analytical estimates of the force–displacement response for out-of-plane loading (Chung et al., 2018) suggest a linear (respectively square root) dependency of the force on the punch radius.

In view of evaluating the safety of electric vehicles, understanding the in-plane response of prismatic cells is also of interest. Kermani et al. (2021) carried out an elastic bucking analysis to quantify the difference between the in-plane and out-of-plane response of a lithium-ion jelly stack. In-plane indentation experiments on rectangular cells require lateral constraints to prevent the global buckling of the cell (Sahraei et al., 2012b). This has been achieved through the use of anti-buckling devices (Lai et al., 2014; Sahraei et al., 2012b) or by positioning the cell between rigid or soft lateral layers (Zhu et al., 2020a). Aside from in-plane indentation, three-point bending also provides partial insight into the in-plane response of prismatic cells (Keshavarzi et al., 2022; Sahraei et al., 2012b, Goodman et al., 2020).

Due to the pronounced property contrast within the layered multi-material nature of lithium-ion cells, highly local deformation mechanisms are observed upon global indentation, such as fragmentation of the current collector, delamination or the excessive shearing of liquid-saturated granular layers (Tancogne-Dejean et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2017a; Zhu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020b). These mechanisms are typically observed post-mortem either by dissecting the cell or via X-ray computed tomography. More detailed insight into the governing deformation and failure mechanisms could be obtained via in-situ synchrotron imaging during experiments on cylindrical cells (Finegan et al., 2018; 2017; 2015). A key characteristic of the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation is the abrupt change in slope observed during loading (e.g. Luo et al., 2017; Kisters et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Tancogne-Dejean et al., 2022). Luo et al. (2017) investigated the local state of the cell layers before and after the inflection point and observed a correlation between the change in slope and the start of the adhesion of ceramic coated onto the separator to the graphite anode. At the macroscopic level, Tancogne-Dejean et al. (2022) observed the formation of shear bands at the boundary of the region below the punch when reaching the inflection point in experiments on large format pouch cells.

Numerical simulations with lithium-ion cells are either based on a detailed description of all layers or on a homogenized modeling approach (Zhu et al., 2018a). While detailed models allow for the exact geometrical representation of the layered cell structure, they require the accurate mechanical characterization of all components (Luo et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2019; 2018b). Such models are able to predict the force–displacement response of batteries with good accuracy. They also provide valuable insight into the deformation and failure mechanisms (Li et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2020; Sahraei et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). However, since the mechanically-relevant length scale of the representative volume element (i.e. a cathode/separator/anode/separator assembly) is orders of magnitude smaller than the thickness of the pouch cell, the computational costs of the detailed modeling approach are exceptionally high. Engineers therefore need to rely on homogeneous-equivalent models that can be used in conjunction with coarse meshes when evaluating the response of large scale structures (e.g. full vehicle crash simulation).

In homogenized material models, the macroscopic stress–strain response of a cell is approximated through a set of phenomenological constitutive equations. Among the plasticity models that are readily available in commercial FE software packages, the MAT_HONEYCOMB from the LS-DYNA material library has been frequently used (e.g. Chen et al., 2020; Keshavarzi et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021). It is a very basic model which may be seen as the superposition of six one-dimensional plasticity models, i.e. a yield stress is defined for each component of the stress tensor while hardening is defined as a function of the corresponding component of the plastic strain tensor. While the model neglects multi-axial effects, it provides a high degree of flexibility which guarantees a good fit for simple loading conditions. Due to the crushable nature of the granular anode and cathode materials, the crushable foam model due to Deshpande and Fleck (2000) has also been employed to describe the homogenous-equivalent response of the jelly-stack (e.g. Li and Zhu, 2020; Sahraei et al., 2012b, 2012a; Tancogne-Dejean et al., 2022). Unlike the uncoupled honeycomb material model, it makes use of equivalent stress and strain measures (that are fully defined by the plastic Poisson’s ratio) to define the hardening. Other candidate models include pressure-dependent anisotropic models (Greve and Fehrenbach, 2012), critical state plasticity model (Wang et al., 2017b) or the Drucker-Prager cap model (Deng et al., 2020). As an alternative to phenomenological modeling, machine-learning based surrogate models of battery cells may also be obtained through computational homogenizations (Tancogne-Dejean et al., 2021).

In the present study, small format pouch cells are mechanically loaded along their in-plane and out-of-plane directions using indenters of different sizes and shapes. Stereo Digital Image Correlation (3D-DIC) is used to monitor the surface displacement fields during out-of-plane hemispherical indentations. After establishing the punch size dependent scaling of the force–displacement curves, numerical simulations of all experiments are carried out using the Deshpande-Fleck plasticity model with two-stage hardening. Based on the comparison of the numerical and experimental results for a wide range of loading conditions, recommendation for modeling the large deformation response of pouch cells in industrial practice are made.

The mechanical behavior of commercially available pouch cells (4.0Ah capacity) is investigated. The pouch cell dimensions are 65.2 mm × 58.6 mm × 5.07 mm (thickness) as shown on Fig. 1 . The cell’s inner jelly stack is made of 17 layers of anode, 18 layers of cathode (including two single-sided layers on the outer surfaces) and 34 layers of separators. The anode is composed of graphite coated on both sides of a copper foil, while the anode is a double-sided coated aluminum foil with active material. For the present cell, the chemistry has not been determined or communicated by the manufacturer, but given the applicational context, it is expected to be Lithium Cobalt Oxide (LCO). The thickness of the anode and cathode layers are 0.145 mm and 0.120 mm respectively, while the separator thickness is around 0.022 mm. The polymer-aluminum composite pouch has a thickness of 0.10 mm. Cathodes and anodes exhibit the cell shape while a layer of separator is wounded along the x-direction. Prior to mechanical testing, the as-received cells are fully discharged following a constant-current constant-voltage procedure at a discharging current of 2A with a target voltage of 3 V.

Fig. 2 shows scanning electron microscopy (Scios, FEI) images of the jelly stack and its components. The separator exhibits randomly dispersed submicron-sized pores. The graphite coating on the anode side is composed of platelet-like particles with a diameter of around 12 µm, while the active material coating on the cathode side exhibits a bimodal grain distribution with particles of diameters around 5 µm and 17 µm.

Hemispherical and cylindrical indentations are performed on the pouch cells up to the onset of short circuit and mechanical failure with various indenter radii and manually stopped. Hemispherical indentations are performed normal to all three outer surfaces of the cell (two in-plane and one out-of-plane testing configurations), while cylindrical indentations are only performed normal to the thickness direction of the rectangular prism (out-of-plane direction). In order to reduce the number of experiments mirror symmetry is assumed with respect to each of the three Cartesian planes. This neglects the possible influence of the two connector tabs on the mechanical response of the pouch cell (jelly stack). All experiments are performed on a universal testing machine (Instron 8810) equipped with either a 10kN or a 100kN load cell. The loading speed for all indentations is set to
0.3 m m / min
, corresponding to an average engineering strain rate of 10−3/s. The open circuit voltage of the cell is monitored synchronously with the force at a frequency of 1 Hz. Two repeats are performed for each loading case.

For out-of-plane indentation experiments, the specimen is positioned between the indenter (hemispherical or cylindrical) and a rigid (non-polished) steel base. The indenter is held stationary, while the rigid plate moves towards the indenter. Hemispherical steel indenters with six distinct radii
R =
2.5 , 5 , 10 , 15 , 25 , 50
m m
are used (see Fig. 3 ) as well as cylindrical steel indenters with three distinct radii
R =
5 , 10 , 15
m m
. Grease is added between the pouch and the indenter to reduce friction. The possible spatial variation of indentation results is assessed by performing indentations at four different locations using the two smallest indenters (i.e.
R =
2.5 , 5
m m
). The locations of indentation are highlighted in Fig. 1a.

The vertical displacement of the moving base plate and the indenter is monitored with a 12MPx camera (Grasshopper GS3-U3-120S6M-C, FLIR) with a pixel size below 24 µm and computed using Digital Image Correlation (DIC). Furthermore, 3D-DIC is used to monitor the deformation of the top surface of the cell during the hemispherical indentation. For this, images are recorded by two 9MPx cameras (Grasshopper GS3-U3-91S6M-C, FLIR) with an average pixel size of 30 µm. All cameras are equipped with 50 mm lenses (Nikkor 50 mm f/1.8); the recording frequency is set to 1 Hz and is synchronous to the force and voltage monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reported indenter displacement, identification of onset of short circuit
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the low initial force level and the possible geometrical imperfections of the cells (uneven pouch cell surfaces), it is difficult to determine the instant at which the initial contact between the cell and the indenter occurs. In the present study, this instant (and thus the zero point of the reported indenter displacement) is defined based on a regression of the initial force–displacement curve. This method allows for a characterization independent of the indenter radius (and observed force level). The beginning of the curves (between a minimum force of
F min
= 50 N
and a quarter of the maximum force
F max
> 1000 N
) is fitted by a power law
F
fit
= a
u -
u 0
n
with offset
u 0
. The cost function reads:
(1)
φ
a ,
u 0
, n
=
1
N p
∑
F min
< F < 0.25 ∗
F max
F
fit
- F
F
2
where u is the relative displacement of the rigid support and the indenter displacement (obtained via DIC); F is the measured force.
N p
represents the number of experimental data points within the interval
F min
< F < 0.25
F max
. The final displacement shifted to its origin the reads:
(2)
U = u -
U 0
where
A ,
U 0
, N
= arg min
φ
. The choice of the origin is dictated on the lower end by the resolution of the load cell and on the upper end by the maximum force measured during the indentation.

The onset of short circuit is defined as the instant where a first drop of the voltage is observed. At the beginning of the experiment (up to
0.25
F max
) the voltage has a mean value around 3.5 V and a standard deviation of less than 1 mV; the onset of short circuit is defined as the instant where the voltage drops by more than five standard deviations, i.e. by about 5 mV for the pouch cells tested here. Finally, the force–displacement curves are truncated when the force decreases at a rate higher than 80kN/mm.

After mechanical indentation, optical micrographs of selected cells are acquired. The cells are cut with a disk saw (Labotom 5, Struers) along a plane parallel to the direction of loading (i.e. along the x-z-plane for the out-of-plane indentation).

Finite element simulations of the indentation experiments are carried out using the finite element software Abaqus/Explicit. The punch and support plates are modeled using analytical rigid surfaces. The cell is discretized using first-order hexahedral elements with reduced integration (type C3D8R of the Abaqus element library). The minimum element size is set to 0.18 mm for the in-plane directions, while 35 elements are used along the thickness direction of the pouch cells (see Fig. 5
). The soft skin of the pouch cell is not included in the FE model. A kinematic contact algorithm is used with a friction coefficient of 0.1 to model the cell/metal and cell/cell contact.

The simulations are performed on quarter cell models using the symmetry of the in-plane and out-of-plane loading scenarios. Note that different quarters of the pouch cell are chosen depending on the direction of loading. For out of-plane loading (along the z-axis), symmetry boundary conditions are prescribed for nodes on y-z- and x-z-planes (full thickness is modeled). For in-plane loading (along the x-axis), symmetry boundary conditions are prescribed for nodes on the x-y- and x-z-planes (only half of the thickness is modeled). Furthermore, a second cell (red color in Fig. 5b) is positioned below the specimen (green color in Fig. 5b). Preliminary simulations revealed no noticeable differences between the different positions of the indenter.
Mass scaling is used to reduce the computational time for the slow and intermediate speed simulations (with a minimum of 600,000 critical time increments guaranteeing quasi-static equilibrium).

We briefly recall the basic constitutive equations of a phenomenological model that describes the homogeneous-equivalent large deformation response of a battery cell. The jelly-stack is assumed to be elastically-isotropic with a Young’s modulus E and a Poisson’s ratio ν . Vector notation is used to denote the components of the Cauchy stress tensor and the energy-conjugate strain increment tensor,
(3)
σ
=
σ 11
,
σ 22
,
σ 33
,
σ 13
,
σ 23
,
σ 12
T
(4)
d
ε
p
=
d
ε
11
p
, d
ε
22
p
, d
ε
33
p
, 2 d
ε
13
p
, 2 d
ε
23
p
, 2 d
ε
12
p
T

The elastic domain limit is described by the isotropic Deshpande and Fleck yield surface (Deshpande and Fleck, 2000),
(5)
f =
σ
¯
σ
- k
where the equivalent stress can be expressed through a quadratic form.
(6)
σ
¯
=
(
P σ
) ·
σ

The yield surface shape is determined through a single parameter
P 12
. We note that the von Mises field function is recovered for
P 12
= - 0.5
, while convexity is guaranteed for
- 0.5 ⩽
P 12
⩽ 0.5
(Deshpande and Fleck, 2000). The flow rule is chosen to be associated, leading to the evolution equation.
(8)
d
ε
p
=
d
ε
¯

σ
¯
P σ

with
d
ε
¯
p
denoting the work-conjugate equivalent plastic strain increment. With the help of Eq., we can also evaluate the plastic Poisson’s ratio, which yields
ν p
= -
P 12
.

The deformation resistance is chosen as a sum of an initial resistance
k 0
and the strain hardening term
Δ k
,
(9)
k
[
ε
¯
p
]
=
k 0
+ Δ
k ε
[
ε
¯
p
]

A two-stage hardening law comprised of an initially convex-up power law followed by a concave-shaped exponential law (following Li and Zhu, 2020). In order to avoid numerical instabilities related to almost perfect plasticity at large strain, a linear term is added to the exponential hardening term. The transition between both laws occurs at a critical strain
ε c
.
Enforcing C1-continuity of the deformation resistance, the two-stage hardening law reads.
(10)
Δ
k ε
[
ε
¯
p
]
=
A
ε
¯
p
n
if
ε
¯
p
⩽
ε c
A
ε c n
+ B
ε
¯
p
-
ε c
+ Q
1 - exp
- β
ε
¯
p
-
ε c
if
ε
¯
p
>
ε c

with the power law parameters
A , n
, the exponential law parameter Q , the linear hardening term B , and the dependent parameter β.
(11)
β =
An
ε c
n - 1
- B
Q

The deformation resistance is then fully described by six parameters
k 0
,
ε c
, A , n , Q , B
.

The material model outlined above has been implemented in a user material subroutine (VUMAT) using a basic return-mapping algorithm (Simo and Hughes, 1998). It comprises nine material parameters including the two elastic constants, the plastic Poisson’s ratio defining the shape of the yield surface and the six hardening parameters. The Young’s modulus of
E = 4200 M P a
is taken from previous works by Wierzbicki and coworkers (e.g. Chung et al., 2018; Li and Zhu, 2020). The elastic and plastic Poisson’s ratio are set to
ν =
ν p
= 0.1
. As proposed by Tancogne-Dejean et al. (2022), the initial yield stress is set to
k 0
= 0.1 M P a
; a smooth Voce transition is obtained when choosing
Q = 1 M P a
. The four remaining hardening law parameters are calibrated based on the experimentally-observed force–displacement response of the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with a radius of 10 mm. A gradient-free optimization method (Nelder and Mead, 1965) is used to minimize the normalized error between the experimentally-obtained and simulated force–displacement curves.

The force displacement and voltage displacement curves for all out-of-plane hemispherical indentations are presented in Fig. 6 . For all diameters, the force–displacement curve is initially convex and transitions towards a concave shape before reaching a maximum followed by a steep drop in force. According to our voltage recordings, the short circuit occurs slightly before the force maximum (up to 0.3 mm earlier). Until that instant, the voltage remains approximately constant. The force–displacement curves exhibit a slight dependency on the location of indentation (see Fig. 7 a and b) with positions 2, 3 and 4 featuring around 10 % lower maximum force (i.e. 0.1kN and 0.3kN for the indenter radii 2.5 mm and 5 mm, respectively) and 5 % lower displacement at failure (i.e. 0.05 mm and 0.1 mm for the indenter radii 2.5 mm and 5 mm, respectively). During the experiments with the smallest indenter, pouch rupture was observed below the indenter.

According to the analytical estimate by Chung et al. (2018) the force scales linearly with the punch radius and the cell thickness H , i.e.
F ∝ R H
. To compare the results from experiments with different indenter sizes, we plotted the normalized-force (
F / R
H
) -displacement curves in Fig. 8 a. With the exception of the smallest indenter (
R = 2.5 m m
), all curves lie approximately on top of each other. This holds true for the overall shape of the curve, its maximum in force (Fig. 8b) and for the displacement at the onset of short circuit (Fig. 8c). The normalized maximum force is around 80 MPa for the smallest indenter, while it lies around 150 MPa for the radii 10, 15 and 20 mm and attains about 180 MPa for the two largest indenters (see Fig. 8b). The displacement at the onset of short circuit is almost about 1.85 mm for all indenter sizes except for the smallest one, which causes final failure at a displacement of about 1.1 mm (see Fig. 8c).

A characteristic feature of the hemispherical indentation along the out-of-plane direction of the cell is the abrupt change of the F-u-slope in the large deformation regime. Fig. 6 shows the slope of the force–displacement curves (gray lines) obtained through numerical differentiation (finite-difference scheme). With the exception of the indentation with the smallest punch, all experiments exhibit a slope maximum followed by a drop of around 25 % of the maximum at a displacement between 0.8 mm and 1 mm. Furthermore, for the two largest indenter radii (
R =
25 , 50
m m
), the slope curves exhibit multiple local peaks (see for example Fig. 6e at 0.9, 1.2 and 1.4 mm).

Fig. 10a shows the displacement of the top surface of the cell during the indentation (also shown on the right axis of Fig. 6). The measurements exhibit good repeatability. As visible in Fig. 6, the rise is correlated with the slope change, including the multiple slope peaks visible for the largest two indenters. The initial negative values of the displacement correspond to slight geometrical discrepancies between cells and positioning uncertainties which never exceed 0.12 mm (i.e. 2 % of the cell thickness). The average surface displacement speed is
0.12 , 0.20 , 0.36 , 0.46 , 0.61 , 0.81
m m / m i n
for increasing indenter radii. It should be noted that for the indenter radii greater than 5 mm, the vertical upward motion of the cell surface is faster than the downward moving indenter; in our experiments, it is up to three times faster. The displacement at which the cell surface starts moving upwards decreases from 1 mm for the smallest two indenters to 0.87 mm for the intermediate radii and to 0.76 mm for the largest one.

A surface profile of the bottom surface of the cells have been obtained after the experiment using the same 3D-DIC setup. The final shape of the bottom surface is composed of two planes, one beneath the indenter and one outside the indented area (see Fig. 9e) with an annular transition region. For example, for the intermediate radius
R = 10 m m
, the vertical offset between the two planes is equal to 1.45 mm, while the radius of the top circle is 6.7 mm. It is noteworthy that the offset between the two planes is equal to the vertical displacement of the top surface. Thus, the vertical rigid body displacement of the top surface is directly related to the formation of an annular region undergoing (out-of-plane) shear deformation.

Fig. 11 summarizes the results for the out-of-plane cylindrical indentations for the three cylinder radii
R =
5 , 10 , 15
m m
. As for the hemispherical indentations, the force–displacement curve is initially convex and transitions towards a concave shape before reaching a maximum force followed by a sudden force drop. For the cylindrical indentations, the force drop and the onset of short circuit occur simultaneously. According to analytical estimates of Chung et al. (2018), the force has a square root dependence on the indenter radius and cell thickness, while scaling linearly with the width W of the indented pouch cell, i.e.
F ∝ W
RH
. The normalized force (
F /
W
RH
) -displacement curves (Fig. 11d) are almost identical, confirming the general scaling obtained from the power law hardening assumed by Chung et al. (2018). The normalized maximum force is about 94 MPa and the displacement at fracture attains 1.1 mm.

The force–displacement curves obtained from cylindrical indentations do not exhibit any abrupt slope change; they transition smoothly from convex to concave between 0.5 mm and 0.75 mm. For the cylindrical indentations, we also used 2D-DIC to monitor the displacement of the cells’ sides during the indentation. Fig. 12 shows the evolution of the side profile along with DIC images at displacement of 0.5 mm, 1 mm and at maximum force (1.10 mm). Away from the indenter, the side rises in a way that the top surface remains approximately flat, suggesting a rigid body rotation of the cell region away from the punch. Fig. 11 summarizes the angular rotation of both sides of the cell (blue lines). For all indenters’ radii, the cell rotates slowly during the convex force evolution (up to around 0.6 mm) and rotates faster later. It is worth noting that both sides of the cells (full line and dashed lines) show similar evolution during loading. The maximum rotation increases as a function of the indenter radii, from around 2° to around 2.8° for the biggest indenter. In specimens loaded with cylindrical indenters, we could not detect any localized shear zone. Instead, we observe a smooth transition in the deformation field from the compression zone under the indenter to the freely rotating edges.

The force–displacement curves for the in-plane hemispherical indentations are shown in Fig. 13 . For comparison purposes, these plots include black lines that show the result for out-of-plane indentation performed in the center of the cell. The in-plane indentation experiments exhibit a lower repeatability than their out-of-plane counterparts which is attributed to friction (between the cells and with the lateral support structure) and a higher waviness of the indented cell walls. Repeated drops in force are seen that are reminiscent of a stick–slip type of mechanism that may be caused by friction. Overall, the in-plane deformation response is vastly different from that for out-of-plane loading.

The maximum forces attained are about two times higher for in-plane loading and the displacements at the onset of short-circuit are larger. Specifically, the onset of short circuit for an indentation along the long cell side (position 5, purple dot in Fig. 13) is reached at a displacement of around 4 mm and at a force greater than 1.4kN. Lower values are observed for the indentation along the short side of the cell (position 5, green dot in Fig. 13). For the two repeat experiments, we have a displacement at the onset of short circuit of 2.2 mm and 3.5 mm, respectively, and forces of 0.8kN and 1.1kN, respectively. Comparing both in-plane indentations, the indentation along the cell short side exhibits initially higher forces with larger slopes (up to 3 mm) and then a lower force level. The differences are tentatively attributed to the winding of the jelly-stack.

The voltage shows a sharper decrease for indentation along the long side of the cell. Postmortem observation shows a rupture of the pouch for all in-plane indentations and the sharper voltage drop could also be caused by the contact between the indenter and the metallic current collectors, while the separator winded on the short cell sides may delay the rapid electrical contact after pouch failure.

Fig. 14 shows photographs of cells that have been cut after hemispherical indentations. It is worth noting that upon cutting the cell with a disc saw, the vacuum holding the jellystack and the pouch together is released leading to the creation of gaps between the layers. For the smallest indenter (Fig. 14a), the inelastic deformation does not propagate through the entire cell thickness and no clear shear band or failure can be observed. Upon increasing the indenter radii (Fig. 14b-f), the vertical displacement of the cell is visible away from the indenter while a sheared region is observed. Furthermore, failure can be observed in the form of foil rupture (Fig. 14b) or shear band (see Fig. 14d). For the largest indenter (Fig. 14f), the cell sides are no longer planar.

Fig. 14g-h present the micrographs for the specimens that were subject to in-plane indentations. In both cases, shear and kink bands are observed at a distance from the side that is larger than the total indenter displacement. The pouch is punctured and indentation of the jelly-stack can be observed. It should be noted that the prescribed “soft” confinement (with adjacent pouch cells) allows for the indented cell to bulge outwards.

As detailed in Section 3, the hardening law is identified based on the experimental result for out-of-plane indentation with a hemispherical punch of radius
R = 10 m m
. The obtained hardening curve is plotted in Fig. 4. All other parameters of the constitutive model are summarized in Table 1 .

After using one experiment for model parameter identification, we can still use the remaining eight for validating the identified model. The force–displacement curves obtained from numerical simulations and experiments are compared in Fig. 15 for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentations. The agreement for the calibration experiment (Fig. 15c) is very good up to the onset of failure. For the two smaller indenters, the numerical simulations slightly overestimate the experimental curves, while the simulations tend to underestimate the force for indentations with larger punch radii. All predictions are close to the experimental results with the exception of the indentation with
R = 50 m m
, which deviates from the experimental results by about 20 % at a displacement of 1.5 mm. It is noteworthy that no sharp slope change occurs. For the cylindrical indentations (Fig. 16 ), the agreement is on par with the predictions for hemispherical indentations. This confirms the capability of the simulation model to capture the force–displacement response of the cell under out-of-plane indentation.

The simulation results for the in-plane indentations are shown in Fig. 17 . The numerical simulations are able to approximate the experimentally-measured force–displacement response with reasonable accuracy. The simulation for the indentation of the short side (loading 6) exhibits a slightly higher force (+7%) compared to the loading of the long side. This is solely due to the change in boundary conditions (side length) as the constitutive model is fully-isotropic. The same holds true for the differences between in-plane and out-of-plane indentation. This emphasizes the primary role of boundary conditions on the indentation response rather than anisotropic material effects. Given the small discrepancies between the experiments and the simulation results, it may be concluded that a simple isotropic model of a battery cell is able to provide reasonable predictions of the global force–displacement responses for indentation along different directions.

Fig. 18 shows the stress field under the hemispherical indenter for all out-of-plane indentations at maximum force. The equivalent stress (as defined by Eq. (6)) reaches about 102 MPa for all radii just under the punch which is in the plateau-like region of the hardening curve. The stress field below the smallest indenter shows the highest gradient along the thickness direction reaching around 60 MPa at the bottom of the cell. For larger radii, the region under the indenter exhibits a more uniform equivalent stress distribution. As far as the predicted displacement field is concerned, we note that the vertical displacement of the cell corner (black dot on Fig. 5) attains
0.08 , 0.28 , 0.64 , 0.90 , 1.18 , 1.30
m m
(for increasing punch radii) when the indenter reaches the position where failure occurs in the experiments. These values are significantly smaller (by up to 72 %) than their experimentally-measured counterparts.

The stress field after in-plane indentation to a displacement of 4.2 mm is shown on Fig. 21 . Due to the large ratio between the cell length and the indenter radius, the stress field is reminiscent of that of Hertzian contact for point load in an infinite medium, with drop-like stress isocontour. This means that the loaded region is much larger than in the out-of-plane indentation. It is noteworthy that the external cells used for lateral constraint exhibits regions with equivalent stress above the yield stress, indicating permanent deformation of the cells due to the lateral expansion of the central cell (as experimentally seen in Fig. 14g and h). However, no kink or shear bands are observed (compare with Fig. 14 g and h).

The stress state evolution right under the indenter and at the mid-thickness is shown on Fig. 22 for all out-of-plane indentation. The stress triaxiality η and the Lode angle parameter
θ
¯
are defined as:
(12)
η =
1 3
I 1
3
J 2

and
(13)
θ
¯
= 1 -
2 π
arccos
3
3
2
J 3
J 2
3 / 2
where
I 1
,
J 2
,
J 3
are the first invariant of the stress tensor and the second and third invariants of the deviatoric stress tensor, respectively. For all loading cases and indenter radii, the stress state right under the punch is characterized by an almost proportional loading with a triaxiality of −0.45 and a Lode angle parameter of −1, close to a state of uniaxial compression (
η = -
1 / 3
,
θ
¯
= - 1
). This is due to the small values of the elastic and plastic Poisson’s ratio, preventing lateral expansion. At the cell mid-thickness, the stress state evolves for both loading cases and for the indenter radii. For hemispherical indentations, the Lode angle parameters remain at −1, however the triaxiality increases with decreasing indenter radii, up to a value close to 0 for the smallest indenter. The change from a convex to a linear hardening at an equivalent plastic strain of
ε c
leads to an increase in the triaxiality. For the cylindrical indentations, the Lode angle parameter stays close to −0.7 at the cell mid-thickness with a triaxiality around −0.35. It should be noted that the small stress state dependency introduced by the present constitutive modeling (in particular the choice of the yield surface) prevents the direct use of a stress-state dependent fracture criterion, explaining the large difference in the displacement at failure under hemispherical and cylindrical indentations.

The results suggest that a rather simple constitutive model of the jelly-stack is able to provide meaningful engineering estimates on the indentation response of pouch cells. The modeling includes many strong simplifications including the assumption of an isotropic material response. Due to the layered nature of the jelly-stack in combination with a high property contrast of the constituent materials (metallic, polymeric and granular), a highly orthotropic homogenized stress–strain response could be expected. However, in the case of compressive loading, the loading direction dependency turned out to be weak. This can be explained by the slenderness of the metallic foils comprised in a pouch cell. The length of the few micron thick foil layers is about 60 mm, which equates to a length-to-thickness ratio of the order of 103(!). The current collector foils are thus expected to buckle immediately upon in-plane compressive loading despite the partial confinement through the adjacent granular layers. As a result, they do not contribute to the in-plane compressive loading capacity of the jelly-stack. Instead, the in-plane loads are carried by the saturated granular cathode and anode materials. In case of out-of-plane compressive loading, the stresses are transmitted through the foils, but they are not expected to contribute to the overall through thickness deformation. In other words, the out-of-plane response is also expected to be governed by the granular cathode and anode materials. For a low volume fraction of metallic foils in the jelly stack (around 10 %), the macroscopic compressive response of battery cells may thus be expected to be similar for the in-plane and out-of-plane directions, which is consistent with the obtained good quality of the force–displacement curve predictions that have been obtained with our simple isotropic plasticity model.

The hemispherical indentations are characterized by an abrupt change in the slope of the force–displacement curves simultaneous to a vertical cell motion. This response is attributed to a change in deformation mechanism. Initially, the slope of the force–displacement curve is increasing because of the increasing contact area of the punch, and the compaction of the granular anode and cathode materials below the indenter. During that initial phase, the problem may be seen as the local indentation of a semi-infinite medium. Even though there is a build-up of radial and circumferential tensile stresses in the top layers, the bending and shear resistance of the sandwich formed by the straight lower layers is still sufficiently high to prevent any global deformation mode (in the plastic range). Once this resistance for plastic deformation of the entire specimen cross-section is overcome, the global deformation mode is activated as well (in addition to the local indentation). In our measurements, the global deformation mode is detected through the vertical motion of the edges.

Regarding cylindrical indentations, the indented area evolves with the square-root of the displacement. As for the hemispherical indentation, this area increase along with the material hardening leads to an increase of the slope of the force–displacement curve at the beginning of the loading. However, in the cylindrical indentation, the global deformation mode is activated much earlier. This is due to the fact that the lifting of the shear-soft entire layup is already possible when the critical bending moment for the metallic current collectors is overcome. In the case of the hemispherical indenters, there is also a hoop strain (tension) that accompanies the global deformation mode. As a result, the threshold for activating the global deformation mode is much lower for cylindrical indentation than hemispherical indentation. As a consequence, the edges begin to lift up at an early stage of the experiment. The decreasing slope is attributed to the non-linear compressive response of anode material. As shown by Zhu et al. (2019), the strain hardening of the pressure-volumetric strain relationship for the porous anode material decreases substantially as the strains exceed 0.2.

The onset of short circuit in lithium-ion cells is related to the physical contact of cathode and active coating materials. A typical assumption is that the intimate contact between the cathode and anode coating material occurs after the failure of the separator. However, several other damage and failure mechanisms are at play in this complex structure. Zhu et al. (2018b) investigated the deformation mechanisms of anode, cathode and separator foils using micro-testing and reveals early particle cracking and debonding for the active materials. At the cell level and under hemispherical indentation, Zhu et al. (2020b) revealed the presence of radial crack in the copper foils using X-ray tomography. Shear bands are also observed which could span the entire cell height (see Fig. 14 as well as Chung et al. 2018, Tancogne-Dejean et al. 2022). In the present study, small voltage drops occur between the onset of short-circuit and up to global mechanical failure; these voltage drops are correlated with drops in force. It is postulated that two mechanisms are at play relating the voltage and force changes: i) when the shear band propagates, it occurs in a series of cracks and arrests leading to small force drops upon crack propagation. Propagation of these cracks increases the contact area between the anode and cathode and thus decreases the open circuit voltage. ii) On the other hand, a small force decrease can lead to elastic unloading of the cell resulting in smaller contact areas in short-circuited regions. This can happen prior to the onset of a global shear band due to the reorganization of the granular media. An almost instantaneous voltage drop to 0 V is observed for the cylindrical indentations further emphasizing the influence of the shear band formation on the short circuit occurrence.

The present study focuses of commercially available lithium-ion cells. The structural (and chemical) similarities between portable electronics and electric vehicle pouch cells should allow transposing the results to these larger cells. However, significantly different results are expect for solid-state cells due to the substantial material changes, (e.g. the substitution of the granular graphite at the anode by a bulk lithium metal foil, or of the polymeric separator by a ceramic electrolyte).

An extensive experimental campaign has been performed on small-format 4Ah lithium-ion pouch cells, composed of out-of-plane and in-plane indentations with nine different indenters. The use of several indenter radii reveals that the force–displacement response (for indenter radii larger than the cell thickness) scales linearly with the radius of the hemispherical indenters. For experiments with cylindrical indenters, the responses scales with the square root of the cylinder radius. For the in-plane indentation experiments, the pouch cell specimens are sandwiched between two cells to mimic the boundary conditions of cells comprised in battery packs.

The surface deformation fields were monitored using 2D- and 3D-Digital Image Correlation. It reveals a correlation between the abrupt change in slope of the force–displacement curve and a vertical lift of the free edges of the pouch cells. Post-mortem evaluation shows that the lift occurs due to the presence of localized shear in an annular region of intense shearing around the indenter. For cylindrical indentations, the rotation of the undeformed cell material around the indenter increases with the indenter radius.

Finite element simulations are performed of all experiments using an enhanced Deshpande-Fleck porous plasticity model with self-similar two-stage hardening to describe the large deformation response of the jelly-stack. It is found that the simple isotropic model provides reasonable predictions of the force–displacement responses for in-plane and out-of-plane indentation. This result is unexpected because of the highly orthotropic nature of the jelly-stack that is not taken into account by an isotropic Deshpande-Fleck model. We argue that due to the high slenderness of the metallic foils comprised in a pouch cell, they buckle early and do not contribute to the in-plane load carrying capacity. As a result, the macroscopic compressive response of the jelly-stack is governed by the saturated granular anode and cathode materials which explains the successful predictions of the isotropic porous plasticity model.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Paul Meyer (ETH) for his help in performing the experiments. Dr. Christian Roth (ETH) and Prof. Vincent Grolleau (ETH) are thanked for valuable discussions. The support of MIT’s Industrial Battery Consortium is also gratefully acknowledged.
